flashcards.Back: The most common type of copper cabling used in Ethernet networks. It consists of four pairs of color-coded wires twisted together to reduce electromagnetic interference.
An optical cable designed to transmit a single ray of light over very long distances (up to 100km). It uses a thin 9-micron core and a laser light source.
An optical cable that allows multiple paths of light to travel through a large core (50 or 62.5 microns). it is used for shorter distances within buildings and uses LED sources.
The standard physical connector used for terminating twisted-pair Ethernet cables. It contains eight pins that align with the eight internal wires of the UTP cable.
A hot-swappable modular transceiver used to connect switch ports to fiber or copper cabling. It allows for easy hardware upgrades without replacing the entire switch.
An enhanced UTP cable standard that supports speeds up to 1,000 Mbps (Gigabit Ethernet) at distances up to 100 meters. it significantly reduced crosstalk compared to original Cat 5.
A UTP cable standard that supports 10 Gbps speeds for short distances (up to 55 meters) and 1 Gbps for up to 100 meters. it includes a plastic separator to reduce crosstalk.
An augmented version of Cat 6 that supports 10 Gbps speeds for the full 100-meter length. it features improved shielding and tighter wire twisting.
A legacy network environment where multiple devices compete for access to the same physical cable. It relies on CSMA/CD to manage collisions and is rarely used in modern switched networks.
A direct physical or logical link between exactly two nodes. in modern Ethernet, this refers to a full-duplex connection between a switch and a host or another switch.
The IEEE 802.3ab standard for Gigabit Ethernet over copper UTP cabling. It requires all four pairs of wires and supports distances up to 100 meters.
The IEEE standard for Gigabit Ethernet over fiber optic cabling using long-wavelength lasers. It supports distances up to 5km over single-mode fiber.
The IEEE standard for Gigabit Ethernet over multimode fiber using short-wavelength lasers. It is commonly used for backbone links within a single building (up to 550m).
Disturbance caused by external electrical sources that can degrade data signals in copper cabling. UTP uses twisting to cancel this out, while STP adds a metal shield.
A type of interference where electrical signals from one wire pair leak into an adjacent pair within the same cable. High-quality categories and precise twisting minimize this effect.
An Ethernet cable where the wire pins on both ends match exactly. It is used to connect dissimilar devices, such as a computer to a switch or a switch to a router.
An Ethernet cable where the transmit and receive pairs are swapped at one end. It is traditionally used to connect similar devices, like two switches or two computers.
A switch feature that automatically detects the required cable type (straight-through or crossover) and configures the interface accordingly, eliminating the need for manual crossover cables.
The gradual loss of signal strength as data travels through a medium. It is measured in decibels and is the primary factor limiting the maximum length of a network cable.
The central glass or plastic strand of an optical fiber that carries the light signal. Its diameter determines whether the fiber is classified as single-mode or multimode.
The outer layer of glass surrounding the fiber core. It has a lower refractive index, causing light to reflect back into the core via total internal reflection.
The working group and collection of standards that define the physical layer and data link layer's media access control (MAC) for wired Ethernet.
The maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a network medium, typically measured in bits per second (bps). Higher categories of cabling offer greater bandwidth.
The specific assignment of internal wires to the individual pins of a connector (like RJ-45). Standard pinouts include T568A and T568B.
Gigabit Interface Converter. A legacy, larger form-factor transceiver module used to connect switch ports to different types of media. It has been replaced by the smaller SFP.
An enhanced version of the SFP transceiver that supports data rates up to 10 Gbps. It has the same physical dimensions as a standard SFP.
A type of copper cabling that includes a metal foil or braid shield around the wire pairs to provide superior protection against electromagnetic interference.
The maximum effective length for all standard copper Ethernet segments (10Base-T through 10GBase-T). Exceeding this limit leads to excessive attenuation and data errors.

The most commonly used wiring standard for terminating RJ-45 connectors in modern commercial network installations. It specifies the sequence: Orange/White, Orange, Green/White, Blue, Blue/White, Green, Brown/White, Brown.
A standardized set of transmission speeds for fiber optic networks (e.g., OC-3, OC-12). It defines the hierarchy of bandwidth used in high-capacity service provider backbones.
